items.title: ssik 1.1 — analytical inverse kinematics for 6R / 7R arms (PyPI, Python)
items.summary:
Hello ROS community,

I’d like to share ssik, a Python library for analytical inverse kinematics on 6R and 7R revolute manipulators. Version 1.1 just shipped to PyPI, and I’m excited for you all to use it. I’d be happy to answer questions, take requests for specific arms, or help anyone integrate it into their stack.

items.title: 2026 State of Cloud Robotics Survey
items.summary:
Hello, robotics developers!

If you’re using cloud robotics at all, we (the Cloud Robotics Working Group) would appreciate you taking the time to fill out a survey.

We’re gathering data on how cloud robotics is used, what it’s doing well, and what still needs improving.

The survey link is here:

items.title: Built a flight recorder for ROS 2 robots — looking for brutal feedback
items.summary:
Hey ROS community,

We’ve been building something called **BlackBox** — think of it like a flight data recorder for your ROS 2 robots.

The idea: every time your robot does something unexpected in the field, you shouldn’t have to dig through raw logs hoping you can reproduce it. BlackBox lets you record **episodes** (a bounded window

items.title: Robotics integrator — autonomous pallet jack bring-up (1–2 week contract, on-site Los Angeles)
items.summary:
What this is: We’re retrofitting a standard 24V electric walkie pallet truck into an autonomous

unit. The autonomy software is already written and runs in simulation — your job is the hardware

bring-up: drive-by-wire, sensors, and getting it driving itself in a cordoned area. This is

integration and fabrication, not writing

items.title: Ollama v0.30.1: llm: ignore llama-server SSE ping comments (#16443)
items.summary: llama.cpp b9478 added a default 30s SSE ping that emits colon-only comment frames (":\n\n") while streamed requests are idle; Ollama treated non-data SSE lines as JSON, so skip SSE comments in completion and chat streams.

items.title: LiteLLM v1.85.3
items.summary: Verify Docker Image Signature All LiteLLM Docker images are signed with cosign . Every release is signed with the same key introduced in commit 0112e53 . Verify using the pinned commit hash (recommended): A commit hash is cryptographically immutable, so this is the strongest way to ensure you are using the original signing key: cosign verify \ --key https://raw.githubusercontent.com/BerriAI/litellm/0112e53046018d726492c814b3644b7d376029d0/cosign.pub \ ghcr.io/berriai/litellm:v1.85.3 Verify using

items.title: Industrial Software Leaders Build Secure, Autonomous AI Engineers With NVIDIA NemoClaw

items.title: NVIDIA Partners With Microsoft on Unified Stack for Agentic AI Deployment, From Windows Devices to Cloud to Local
items.summary: The agentic AI moment has arrived, but delivering on its promise requires more than good models. It also takes fast hardware, secure runtimes, a responsive data layer and models tuned for long-running reasoning.
